name: Static, Dynamic, Gradual, and Hybrid Typing
summary: The design axis along which a language places its type checks: ahead of execution (static), at runtime (dynamic), at typed/untyped boundaries with casts or contracts (gradual), or some hybrid. The choice changes compilation strategy, runtime metadata, optimizer assumptions, diagnostic quality, language-server latency — and what soundness guarantees the system can offer.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A static type checker rejects some programs before execution; a dynamic language defers most checks to runtime; a gradual type system deliberately allows typed and untyped regions to coexist, usually with runtime casts or contracts at boundaries. The engineering choice is not simply safety versus flexibility: it also changes compilation strategy, runtime metadata, optimizer assumptions, diagnostic quality, and language-server latency.

The distinguishing axis across implementations is **where the typed-untyped boundary lives** and **what enforces it at runtime**. Statically-typed cores with compile-time-erased types pay zero runtime cost but cannot interoperate with untyped values; dynamically-typed cores pay per-operation tag-checking cost; gradually-typed systems pay cost only at boundary crossings.

sections.title: TypeScript — pragmatic structural typing for JavaScript
sections.content:
TypeScript is an intentionally pragmatic structural type system for JavaScript. Its handbook explicitly documents unsound compatibility choices made to model common JavaScript idioms — bivariant function parameters, unchecked any-typed values, downcasts via type assertions. The language's design philosophy treats unsoundness as a deliberate ergonomic choice, not a bug: programmers can opt into stricter checking via `--strict`, but the default is JavaScript-compatible.

The lesson is that **a gradual type system can be deliberately unsound** when its primary goal is migration, not formal guarantees. TypeScript's adoption shows that pragmatic unsoundness paired with good language-server tooling can succeed at scale; the cost is that programmers cannot assume the type system enforces all invariants without supplementary testing.

sections.title: Typed Racket and migratory typing
sections.content:
Typed Racket is a strong example of *migratory* typing: typed and untyped modules interoperate through contracts, and the type system uses occurrence typing to understand predicates common in dynamic code (`data/occurrence-typing`). The contracts at module boundaries enforce the static type's invariants when an untyped caller crosses into typed code; if the contract is violated at runtime, the untyped side is *blamed* — the diagnostic points at the wrong-shape data, not the typed code that received it.

The Typed Racket lineage establishes that **gradual typing can preserve sound static guarantees** if boundaries enforce contracts, paying the runtime cost only at the boundary rather than at every operation.

sources.label: Migratory Typing: Ten Years Later / Typed Racket

sections.title: Soundness, usefulness, and deliberate unsoundness
sections.content:
A type system may aim for a formal progress-and-preservation theorem, an implementation-guiding informal discipline, or a pragmatic tool that catches many mistakes without proving full soundness. Deliberate unsoundness is sometimes chosen for compatibility, ergonomics, or ecosystem migration.

The trade-off is whether the choice is made explicit: a type checker that is sound by design can support stronger optimizer and runtime assumptions; a pragmatic checker can be easier to adopt but tends to silently promise guarantees it cannot provide unless that limitation is documented.

For language designers, the rule is: **decide soundness policy before users build mental models**. Once programmers expect the type system to catch a class of error, retrofitting soundness is hard; relaxing soundness is even harder because libraries will have come to rely on the implicit guarantees.

sources.label: Migratory Typing: Ten Years Later / Typed Racket
